excerpt: When Satoshi announced Bitcoin on the cryptography mailing list, he got a skeptical reception at best. Cryptographers had seen too many grand schemes by clueless newbies. They tended to be dismissive beyond what was justified. I was more positive — I had long been interested in cryptographic payment schemes, and I had implemented RPOW. So I was eager to give Bitcoin a try.
body:
Source excerpt:
When Satoshi announced Bitcoin on the cryptography mailing list, he got a skeptical reception at best. Cryptographers had seen too many grand schemes by clueless newbies. They tended to be dismissive beyond what was justified. I was more positive — I had long been interested in cryptographic payment schemes, and I had implemented RPOW. So I was eager to give Bitcoin a try.

---

Cited by leaf "RPOW (Hal Finney, 2004)":
Hal Finney built RPOW — Reusable Proofs of Work — and published a working implementation in 2004. The system took Hashcash tokens (which were one-shot anti-spam tickets) and turned them into transferable digital tokens: a user submits a Hashcash token to the RPOW server, the server checks the work and issues an RSA-signed token of equivalent value back, and the new token can be transferred to others or exchanged at the server for a different token of the same value [1]. Coins held value because they encoded a verifiable claim that someone, somewhere, had spent the requisite CPU.

The innovation was a credible solution to the trusted-server problem. The RPOW server ran inside an IBM 4758 secure coprocessor with publicly-attested code: anyone could verify that the server was running exactly the published source, and the server's RSA key could not be extracted by even the operator [2]. The trust model became "trust the IBM hardware attestation and the published code," which was a real advance over "trust the operator's promises." Finney ran the server himself and received the very first Bitcoin transaction from Satoshi in January 2009, and he was the first person to publicly post about running the Bitcoin software.

RPOW is the closest direct engineering precursor to Bitcoin and demonstrates exactly what Bitcoin had to do better. The remaining trust assumption — that the IBM coprocessor and its attestation chain are intact — was real, single-point-of-failure, and gone the day IBM stopped supporting the hardware. Bitcoin removes the central server entirely, replacing it with a peer-to-peer network where every full node verifies the work itself and no operator's hardware needs to be trusted [3]. The RPOW project is also a useful counterexample to the framing that Bitcoin came from nowhere: Finney's 2004 working system implemented most of what Bitcoin needed, except for the consensus layer that Bitcoin's longest-chain rule eventually provided.
